title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
text:
What is Base R?
When people say Base R, they are referring to the core or basic features of the R language (hence the name). These were developed before the tidyverse and include:

Data Types: numeric, integer, character, logical, factor

Data Structures: vectors, matrices, lists, data frames

Basic Operations: indexing/subsetting, arithmetic/logical comparisons
Programming Tools: if-else statements, loops, functions

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
text:
Base R vs. the Tidyverse


Base R

Developed in the early 1990s as part of R’s core language

Includes built-in functions like mean(), length(), and key programming tools like loops, etc.
Offers more direct control, but code can be longer and harder to read



  

Tidyverse

Formally introduced after 2016 as a set of user-friendly packages
Includes ggplot2, dplyr, forcats, tidyr, stringr, readr, lubridate, etc.
Prioritizes readability and consistency, making code cleaner and easier to read

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
section: How Base R and the Tidyverse Work Together
text:
How Base R and the Tidyverse Work Together

The tidyverse is built on base R — it doesn’t replace it

You can combine base R and tidyverse functions in your code

Why we learn both? You’ll often see both styles in real-world scripts and examples








Tip


Use the tidyverse (with some Base R) for most of your daily work, but understand enough Base R to follow and troubleshoot R code!

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
text:
Example Code in Base R
# create two dataframes

twitter <- data.frame(
  user     = c("alice","bob","carlos","diana","emily","lucia","gina", "rick"),
  likes    = c(45, 80, 22, 130, 55, 60, 15, 30),
  retweets = c(10, 25,  5,  50, 12, 20, 30, 58),
  language = c("en","es","en","en","fr","en","en", "en"),
  state    = c("CA","TX","TX","NY","IL","CA","IL", "AZ"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

state_lookup <- data.frame(
  state  = c("CA","TX","NY","IL","AZ"),
  region = c("West","South","Northeast","Midwest","West"),
  vote = c("Dem", "Rep", "Dem", "Dem", "Rep"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
text:
Example Code in Base R
# create new variables in twitter df with total engagement
twitter$tot_likes_retweets <- twitter$likes + twitter$retweets

# create a logical variable TRUE / FALSE for high engagement
twitter$high_engagement <- twitter$tot_likes_retweets >= 80

# create a new df with only rows that match both of these conditions
# df[rows, columns]: code below says [keep selected rows, all columns]
tw <- twitter[twitter$language == "en" & twitter$high_engagement == TRUE, ]

# bring in the second df using merge
tw_full <- merge(tw, state_lookup, by = "state", all = FALSE)

# compute sum of likes + retweets
sum(tw_full$tot_likes_retweets)

# or compute sum of likes + retweets by each category of vote 
tapply(tw_full$tot_likes_retweets, tw_full$vote, sum)

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
section: R Data Types and Data Structures
text:
R Data Types and Data Structures
Data Types and Data Structures are the fundamentals of R: the “ABC” of how R stores and organizes data. They are part of Base R syntax.

Data Type — what kind of value it is

Data Structure — how values are stored and organized

We explore each and explain how they relate!

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
text:
R Data Types
Data Type: describes the kind of value you’re working with.


R has the following main data types (for more see here):

Numeric – numbers, either with or without decimals

Double with decimals (default): 3.14, 2.0

Integer whole numbers: 2L, 100L

Character – text or strings (always in quotes): "hello"
Logical – TRUE or FALSE values: TRUE, FALSE
Factor – categorical values with levels: "low", "medium"

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
text:
R Data Structures
Data Structure: like a container that holds one or more values.


R has the following main data structures (for more see here):

Vector – one-dimensional, all elements same type
v <- c(1, 2, 3)
Matrix – two-dimensional, all elements same type
m <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2)
Dataframe – two-dimensional, columns can have different types
df <- data.frame(name = c("A", "B"), age = c(25, 30))
List – multi-dimensional, holds elements of any type, even mixed
l <- list(num = 1:3, name = c("Sabrina", "Laura"))

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
section: How Data Types and Data Structures Relate
text:
How Data Types and Data Structures Relate
Every single element in R has a data type. That element is stored inside a larger data structure.
Examples:
x <- c(1, 2, 3)      # a vector (structure) of three elements, all numeric values (type) 
y <- list("a", 5)    # a list (structure) of two elements, one character and one numeric value (type)

 







The data structure you use affects what data types you can store:


Not all data structures support every combination of data types. Some allow only one type, while others can store many types together.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
section: How Data Types and Data Structures Relate
text:
How Data Types and Data Structures Relate









Structure
Dimensions
Allow Mixed Types?
Notes




Vector
1D
No
All elements must be the same data type


Matrix
2D
No
All elements must be the same data type


Dataframe
2D
Yes (by column)
Each column is a vector; data types can vary by column


List
Flexible
Yes
Each element can be anything: numbers, text, vectors, even data frames

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
text:
💻 Practice
Run the code below to create several different R objects. Inspect each object by running both class() and str() on it. Pay attention to the output, and note any questions you have:
# create multiple vectors
num_v <- c(1:9)
char_v <- c("hello", "ciao", "hey")
f_v <- factor(c("low", "medium", "high"))
test <- c("hello", 1, 2)

# create two matrices
num_m <- matrix(1:15, nrow = 3, ncol = 5, byrow = TRUE)
char_m <- matrix(c("a", "b", "c", "d"), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)

# create one dataframe
df <- data.frame(
  id = 1:3,
  name = c("Dave", "Ashley", "Rik"),
  age = c(15, 17, 20)) 

# create one list
l <- list(
  num_v = c(1:3),
  m = matrix(1:9, nrow = 3, ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE),
  another_num_v = c(1,2,4),
  char_v = c("Sabrina", "Zach"),
  d = tibble(var_1 = c(1:4),
              var_2 = c(2:5)))

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
section: Why This Matters: Understanding When to Use Each Data Structure
text:
Why This Matters: Understanding When to Use Each Data Structure
Data Frames first!
So far, we’ve been working mostly with data frames — technically with tibbles, the tidyverse version of them.
Data frames are the best structure for working with real-world tabular data in R, and you’ll keep using them throughout the course and beyond.
Strengths of data frames:

Can store columns of different data types (e.g., character, numeric, logical)
Work easily with dplyr, ggplot2, and other tidyverse packages
Make your data easier to manipulate, filter, summarize, and visualize

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
section: Why This Matters: Understanding When to Use Each Data Structure
text:
Why This Matters: Understanding When to Use Each Data Structure
Beyond Data Frames…
But it’s helpful to understand the full set of R data structures, because each has its own strengths:

Vectors: The most basic structure in R; all elements must be the same type.
You are analyzing survey responses and want to store all participants’ ages in a separate vector for further manipulation.
Matrices: Two-dimensional, all elements must be the same type.
You have numeric data that you want to analyze using matrix algebra or linear models.
Lists: Flexible containers that can hold anything — even other lists.
You are analyzing data and want to save multiple outputs (model results, plot, etc.) all in the same data structure.
Data Frames: Two-dimensional, columns can have different types.
You are analyzing survey responses with dplyr, ggplot, or other tidyverse packages.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
section: Example of filtering rows in a dataframe using logical vectors
text:
Example of filtering rows in a dataframe using logical vectors
# load libraries and data
library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)
data(penguins)

# use dplyr filter() to get penguins where body mass is greater than 4000 grams
filtered_penguins <- penguins |> filter(body_mass_g > 4000)
head(filtered_penguins)

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
section: Example of filtering rows in a dataframe using logical vectors
text:
Example of filtering rows in a dataframe using logical vectors
Let’s analyze the previous code to see what’s happening under the hood: R defines a logical vector and applies it to the penguins dataframe:
# use base R to get the column we need: gives a logical vector
filter_vector <- penguins$body_mass_g > 4000

# check its structure
is.vector(filter_vector)
class(filter_vector) 

# use this vector to manually filter the dataframe using base R
# df[rows, columns]: code below says [rows to keep, all columns]
filtered_p <- penguins[filter_vector, ]

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
text:
A particular vector: Scalar
In math a scalar is defined as a single real number but in R, a scalar is simply a vector of length one
Let’s try this code:
# set up a vector x of length 10
x <- sample(10)
x

# add 100 to x using the long way
x + c(100, 100, 100, 100, 100, 100, 100, 100, 100, 100)

# add 100 to x using the "R" way: vector recycling!
x + 100

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
text:
Vector Recycling in R
Let’s see another example. We define two numeric vectors x1 and x2:
# x1 is sequence of numbers from 1 to 2
x1 <- seq(from = 1, to = 2)

# x2 is a sequence of numbers from 1 to 10
x2 <- seq(from = 1, to = 10)

# what happens if we add them?
x1 + x2
What happens? The shorter vector x1 is duplicated five times in order to match the length of the longer vector x2. The same behavior happens for other operations like subtraction, multiplication, logical comparison, etc.






Warning


If the shorter vector is not a multiple of the longer one, R will print a warning message!

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
text:
Vector Recycling in R






Note


This behavior is called Vector Recycling and happens automatically in R: When two vectors of different lengths are used in an operation, R repeats (recycles) the elements of the shorter vector to match the longer vector!



Why It Matters: Vector recycling can cause unexpected results. Check if recycling is what you want R to do — if not, adjust the vector lengths manually, like this:
x1 <- c(1, 2, rep(0, 7))

x1 + x2

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
text:
Subsetting Data Structures in R
There are different ways to access parts of data structures in R.
We’ll cover:

Vectors
Matrices
Data Frames
Lists

Each structure uses square brackets [ ], but how you use them depends on the structure.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
text:
Subsetting Vectors
v <- c(10, 20, 30, 40)

v[2]         # 20 (2nd element)
v[c(1, 3)]   # 10 and 30
v[-1]        # all but the first
v[v > 25]    # elements greater than 25
Vectors are 1D — you just specify positions or logical tests in [ ]

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
text:
Subsetting Matrices
m <- matrix(1:9, nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)

m[1, 2]           # value at row 1, column 2
m[ , 3]           # entire 3rd column
m[2,  ]           # entire 2nd row
m[1:2, 2:3]       # top-right submatrix (rows 1–2, cols 2–3)
m[ , c(1, 3)]     # columns 1 and 3
Matrices are 2D: use [row, column]

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
text:
Subsetting Lists
my_list <- list(
  a = c(20, 30, 40),
  b = c("hello", "ciao"),
  c = TRUE)

my_list[1]         # first element of my_list, by position; returns a list
my_list[[1]]       # content of the first element: 20, 30, 40; returns a vector
my_list[[1]][1]    # first value of the first element: 20

my_list[2]         # second element of my_list, by position; returns a list
my_list["b"]       # second element of my_list, by name; returns a list
my_list$b          # content of the second element, only works by name not by position; returns a vector
my_list[["c"]]     # returns content of "c": TRUE
Remember: use [ ] to extract a sublist from the original list, and [[ ]] or $ to extract actual elements.
What do my_list[["b"]][1] and my_list[[2]][1] return? And what about my_list[["b"]][3] and why?

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
text:
Subsetting Data Frames
df <- data.frame(
  name = c("Alex", "Betty", "Chad", "J"),
  age = c(20, 21, 22, 23))

df[1, ]            # first row (all columns)
df[ , 1]           # first column by index
df[ , "name"]      # first column by label
df$name            # same as above
df[["name"]]       # same as above

df[1, 2]           # single value at row 1, column 2
df[2:3, "name"]    # values in rows 2 and 3 of column name

df[df$age > 20, ]            # filter rows where age > 20
df[df$name == "J", ]         # filter rows where name is "J"
df[df$age == max(df$age), ]  # filter rows with highest age
Each column of a data frame is a vector of a specific data type (e.g., numeric, character, etc.)
As a whole, a data frame behaves similarly to a list: it contains multiple elements of different data types (like a list), but all elements must be the same length (unlike a list).

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 14
section: From the Tidyverse to Base R
text:
From the Tidyverse to Base R
So far, we have learned several core Tidyverse packages:



ggplot2 → graphs

dplyr → data wrangling

tidyr → tidy data

readr → importing/exporting data




forcats → factors

stringr → text/regex

lubridate → dates









Tip


The Tidyverse is often the most intuitive place to start learning R and you’ll keep using it long after this course! But base R helps us understand how R works underneath.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
text:
Control Structures: What’s the Idea?
Up to this point, our R code has been running line by line following in a fixed sequence.








Now we ask: Can our code be dynamic vs fixed?


Example: Can our code skip some lines and run others depending on a condition? Can it repeat itself when needed?
Yes, with control structures!

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
text:
What Are Control Structures?
Control structures let us write code that can:

Make decisions → with conditional statements like if, if...else, etc.
Repeat actions → with loops like for, while

Conditional statements and loops are control structures. Today we focus on conditional statements. Next lecture we focus on loops

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
text:
Conditional Statements
Conditional statements translate the “if this, then that” logic into code: R checks whether a condition is true or false and acts accordingly,
We learn the following:

Single test with if... else If this is true → do this. Otherwise → do that
Multiple tests with if... else if... else If A → do this. Else if B → do that. Else → fallback
Nested tests with one if inside another First check one condition, then check another condition inside it
Vectorized tests with ifelse() Apply “if this, then that” logic to each value in a vector

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
text:

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
text:
1. Single test with “if… else”







Style tip


Do you need the space and {}?
Technically, the code works without them, like this:
age <- 14

if (age > 16) print("You can get a driving license") else print("You cannot drive")
But omitting spaces and {} is considered bad style as it hurts readability and makes errors more likely, especially for more complex statements!

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
section: Note: with conditional statements sequence matters!
text:
Note: with conditional statements sequence matters!







Order matters


Conditional statements are evaluated in order, so the sequence of your code matters:

R checks each condition one by one, from top to bottom

R stops checking once it finds a condition that is TRUE: it runs that block and skips the rest
Order your conditions from most specific to most general, ending with a default case else

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
section: 3. Nested tests by placing one “if” inside another

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
text:
4. Vectorized tests with ifelse()
Our final case for today is ifelse() which looks like a conditional statement, but it’s actually a function that applies a condition to each element of a vector.
Syntax:
ifelse (condition to be evaluated,
        action performed when condition is TRUE,
        action performed when condition is FALSE)
Example:

ifelse(sqrt(16) > 3, 
       sqrt(16),
       0)

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
text:
4. Vectorized tests with ifelse()
Another example, but this time with a vector as input. What is the output of this code?

numbers <- c(10, 6, 7)
ifelse(numbers %% 2 == 1, 
       "odd",
       "even")









How ifelse() works



If the input of ifelse() is a vector, the output is also a vector: ifelse() evaluates each element of the vector and applies the corresponding action
This works because ifelse() supports vectorized operations: operations directly applied on entire vectors, rather than looping through individual elements one-by-one

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
text:
4. Vectorized tests with ifelse()


Regular if / else statements

Can only test one value at a time
Think of them as asking one yes/no question to one person at a time


ifelse()

Can test a whole vector at once
Think of it as asking the same question to everyone in a group at once


Examples:
x <- c(5, 10, 15)

# Using if / else: works only with a single value
if (x > 10) {
  "High"
} else {
  "Low"
}

# Using ifelse(): works element-wise (vectorized)
ifelse(x > 10, "High", "Low")

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
text:
4. Vectorized tests with ifelse()
Because it works element-wise ifelse() it is useful for creating or changing values in a data frame column.
Example: what is the output of this code?

library(tidyverse)

qualify <- tibble("Athlet" = c("Noah", "Julio", "Nick", "Maria"), 
                  "Scores" = c(32, 37, 28, 30))

# using base R 
ifelse(qualify$Scores > 30, 
       "Admitted", 
       "Rejected")

# same using dplyr
qualify %>%
  mutate(status = ifelse(Scores > 30,
                       "Admitted",
                       "Rejected"))

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
text:
4. Vectorized tests with ifelse()
In the previous example, we only had two possible categories: “Admitted” and “Rejected.” What happens to our code if we add a third category: “Waitlisted”?
We could write a nested ifelse() like this:

qualify <- tibble("Athlet" = c("Noah", "Julio", "Nick", "Maria"), 
                  "Scores" = c(32, 37, 28, 30))

qualify <- tibble(
  Athlet = c("Noah", "Julio", "Nick", "Maria"), 
  Scores = c(32, 37, 28, 30))

qualify %>%
  mutate(Status = ifelse(Scores > 35,            # First condition
                         "Admitted",             # If TRUE, assign "Admitted"
                         ifelse(Scores >= 30,    # Second condition (checked only if first is FALSE)
                                "Waitlisted",    # If TRUE, assign "Waitlisted"
                                "Rejected")))    # If both conditions are FALSE, assign "Rejected"

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
text:
4. Vectorized tests with ifelse()
Nested ifelse() statements do the job, but they make the code harder to read.
Compare the previous example with this version that avoids nesting:

qualify <- tibble("Athlet" = c("Noah", "Julio", "Nick", "Maria"), 
                  "Scores" = c(32, 37, 28, 30))

qualify %>%
  mutate(Status = "Rejected") %>%                                  # Start with a default label
  mutate(Status = ifelse(Scores >= 30, "Waitlisted", Status)) %>%  # Update if the condition is met
  mutate(Status = ifelse(Scores > 35, "Admitted", Status))         # Update again if the higher condition met










Tip


When you have 2+ conditions: use separate mutate() + ifelse() steps (as shown here), instead of nesting.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
text:
4. Vectorized tests with ifelse()
Note there is also a tidyverse version of the base R ifelse() function, called if_else() https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/reference/if_else.html
The two are similar, hence I introduced only the base R version here.
In similar past cases (e.g., read_csv() vs. read.csv()), I suggested defaulting to the tidyverse, and that guidance still generally holds. The tidyverse version if_else() does a slightly better job warning us against missteps, but ifelse() is widely used.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 15
text:

text:
4. Using ifelse() to recode variables in a dataframe
You might run into code that uses ifelse() to recode variables in a dataframe.
For example, the variable decisionDirection takes four values: 1 = Conservative; 2 = Liberal; 3 = Other; NA. The code below recodes it to three: 0 = Conservative; 1 = Liberal; NA for both NA and Other.
data |>
  mutate(decision_direction = ifelse(
    decisionDirection == 3, # condition to evaluate
    NA,                     # if true, set to NA
    decisionDirection - 1   # if false, subtract one to value
    ))








Tip


Prefer case_when() over ifelse() for recoding variables: it is easier to read and scale.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
text:
What Are Control Structures?
Control structures let us write code that can:

Make decisions → with conditional statements like if, if...else, etc.
Repeat actions → with loops like for, while

Conditional statements and loops are control structures. Last lecture we focused on conditional statements. Today we focus on loops (specifically for loops), and we continue next lecture.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
section: Tracing Execution in a For Loop
text:
Tracing Execution in a For Loop
Observe this code. What does each run print?

for (i in c(1, 2, 3)) {
  print(i)
  print("Hello")
  my_sum <- 100 + i
  print(my_sum)
  print(paste("End of Loop", i))
}



💻 Change the code and predict what happens
For each change below: start from the original code and clear the environment

Add another print(my_sum) right before my_sum <- 100 + i. What happens?
Add my_sum <- my_sum + i and print(my_sum) right before the last line. What happens?

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
section: Tracing Execution in a For Loop
text:
Tracing Execution in a For Loop
Same code but this time my_sum is defined outside the loop and updated it inside.
Clear the environment and run this code:

my_sum <- 0

for (i in c(1, 2, 3)) {
  print(i)
  print("Hello")
  my_sum <- my_sum + i
  print(my_sum)
  print(paste("End of Loop", i))
}



💻 Change the code and predict what happens
For each change below: start from the original code and clear the environment

Comment out my_sum <- my_sum + i. What happens?
Go back the original code, then move my_sum <- 0 inside the loop after print("Hello"). What happens?

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
text:
Accumulating a Running Total
For loops are very useful for tracking running totals: adding up scores, votes, or anything else one at a time, and tracking how the total grows with each loop.
This is the same code as the previous slide, but simplified. I removed the extra print statements so we focus on one thing: how my_sum updates after each pass through the loop. Run the code:

my_sum <- 0

for (i in c(1, 2, 3)) {
  my_sum <- my_sum + i
  print(my_sum)
}

my_sum


The problem is that this code prints the updated sum value each time, BUT only saves the final value!

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
section: Saving Results from a For Loop
text:
Saving Results from a For Loop
To save all intermediate sum values instead of just the last one, we need to store them in a vector (here output) with one slot per loop iteration. Like this:

my_sum <- 0
output <- vector(mode = "integer", length = 3)  # new!

for (i in c(1,2,3)) {
  my_sum <- my_sum + i
  print(my_sum)
  output[i] <- my_sum     # new!
}

output








output[i] <- my_sum


When i = 1, my_sum = 0 + 1 = 1 → store 1 in output[1] → output becomes: 1 0 0
When i = 2, my_sum = 1 + 2 = 3 → store 3 in output[2] → output becomes: 1 3 0
When i = 3, my_sum = 3 + 3 = 6 → store 6 in output[3] → output becomes: 1 3 6
This saves the running total at each position i in the vector output!

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
section: Saving Results from a For Loop
text:
Saving Results from a For Loop
Our current code works but it’s NOT YET optimal:

It only behaves correctly because the looping input vector is exactly c(1, 2, 3)
If the values in that vector change, the current code may produce incorrect or unexpected results

💻 Try this:
Take the code from the previous slide and change the looping input vector to c(1, 4, 6). Clear the environment and run the code. Observe what happens to output and try to explain why.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
section: Saving Results from a For Loop
text:
Saving Results from a For Loop

my_sum <- 0
output <- vector(mode = "integer", length = 3)

for (i in c(1,4,6)) {
  my_sum <- my_sum + i
  print(my_sum)
  output[i] <- my_sum 
}

output








What Happens in Each Iteration of this Loop


When i = 1 → my_sum = 0 + 1 = 1 → store 1 in output[1] → output becomes: 1 0 0
When i = 4 → my_sum = 1 + 4 = 5 → store 5 in output[4] → output becomes: 1 0 0 5
When i = 6 → my_sum = 5 + 6 = 11 → store 11 in output[6] → output becomes: 1 0 0 5 NA 11
This does not look right! Why?
To see why, we need to understand the difference between looping over elements vs. indexes…

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
section: Saving Results from a For Loop
text:
Saving Results from a For Loop

my_sum <- 0
output <- vector(mode = "integer", length = 3)

for (i in c(1,4,6)) {
  my_sum <- my_sum + i
  print(my_sum)
  output[i] <- my_sum 
}

output








What Happens in Each Iteration of this Loop


When i = 1 → my_sum = 0 + 1 = 1 → store 1 in output[1] → output becomes: 1 0 0
When i = 4 → my_sum = 1 + 4 = 5 → store 5 in output[4] → output becomes: 1 0 0 5
When i = 6 → my_sum = 5 + 6 = 11 → store 11 in output[6] → output becomes: 1 0 0 5 NA 11
This does not look right! Why the output is not [1, 5, 11]?
To see why, we need to understand the difference between looping over elements vs. indexes…

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
section: Elements vs. Indexes: Why It Matters
text:
Elements vs. Indexes: Why It Matters
Run these examples and spot the differences:

# Version A: Looping over elements/values
x <- c(1, 4, 6)
for (i in x) {
  print(i) }    # value

# Version B: Looping over indexes
x <- c(1, 4, 6)
for (i in 1:length(x)) {
  print(i)        # index
  print(x[i]) }   # value at that index

# Same as Version B just using seq_along (recommended)
x <- c(1, 4, 6)
for (i in seq_along(x)) {
  print(i)
  print(x[i]) }








i and x[i]


i is the loop’s index or “counter” and it just counts positions, so it should always go 1, 2, 3, 4, etc.
x[i] is the element or value of the vector x at position i and can be any value

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
section: Elements vs. Indexes: Why It Matters
text:
Elements vs. Indexes: Why It Matters
Let’s see what happens to our previous codes when we save the output instead of just printing:


❌ Version A

x <- c(1, 4, 6)
my_sum <- 0
output <- vector(mode = "integer", 
                 length = length(x))

# looping over elements
for (i in x) {
  print(paste("Current i is:", i))
  my_sum <- my_sum + i
  print(paste("Current sum is:", my_sum))
  output[i] <- my_sum
}

output


✅ Version B

x <- c(1, 4, 6)
my_sum <- 0
output <- vector(mode = "integer", 
                 length = length(x))

# looping over indexes
for (i in seq_along(x)) {
  print(paste("Current i is:", i))
  my_sum <- my_sum + x[i]
  print(paste("Current sum is:", my_sum))
  output[i] <- my_sum
}

output

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
text:
Best Practice: Loop Over Indexes






Tip


Loop over indexes, using for (i in seq_along(x)), instead of looping over elements/values.
Advantages of looping over indexes:

You can modify, save, or assign values by position (e.g., output[i] <- ...) without writing extra code
Your loop works regardless of the values in the vector x
You have full control over both: the position i and the value at that position x[i]
Looping over indexes followw R best practices

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
section: 💻 Practice: Fix the Bug in this For Loop
text:
💻 Practice: Fix the Bug in this For Loop
You are writing for loop to track tomatoes sold over four days. You want to add up the number of tomatoes sold each day, and store the running total in the vector output.
But something is wrong with the code! Can you fix it? First, predict what the output vector should be, then check the code to find the bug.

tomatoes <- c(10, 2, 100, 50) 
total <- 0
output <- vector(mode = "integer", length = length(tomatoes))

for (i in seq_along(tomatoes)) {
  print(paste("Current i is:", i))
  total <- total + i
  print(paste("Current running total is:", total))
  output[i] <- total
}

output

Same Task With and Without a For Loop

library(tidyverse)

data(penguins)



Rows: 344
Columns: 8
$ species           <fct> Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adel…
$ island            <fct> Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgerse…
$ bill_length_mm    <dbl> 39.1, 39.5, 40.3, NA, 36.7, 39.3, 38.9, 39.2, 34.1, …

Task: Calculate the mean value of several columns in this data

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
section: Calculate the mean value of several columns without a loop
text:
Calculate the mean value of several columns without a loop
We can take the mean() function, and apply it to each column:

penguins |> summarize(avg_bill_length = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))
penguins |> summarize(avg_bill_depth = mean(bill_depth_mm, na.rm = TRUE))
penguins |> summarize(avg_flipper_length = mean(flipper_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))
penguins |> summarize(avg_body_mass = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE))


This works, but requires a lot of copy/paste! How can we do the same thing with a for loop?

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
section: Automate the same task with a loop
text:
Automate the same task with a loop
First, initialize an empty vector to store the results.
Second, use a for loop to calculate the mean of each (numeric) column of this dataframe:

output <- vector(mode = "double", length = ncol(penguins))

for (i in seq_along(penguins)) {
  #print(i)
  #print(penguins[i])
  output[i] <- mean(penguins[[i]], na.rm = TRUE)
}

output

Let’s examine this code…

i will index each column
output[i] saves the results in the output vector at the correct position
penguins[[i]] ` pulls out the column’s values as a plain vector

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
text:
For Loop over Columns
So far we’ve looped over vectors, one element at a time.
Now let’s apply the same idea to a data frame. But a data frame is a collection of vectors, that is, columns.
Thus, looping over one with a for loops means looping over its columns.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
section: Observe the same task with and without a loop
text:
Observe the same task with and without a loop
Let’s go back to the penguins:

library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)
data(penguins)



Rows: 344
Columns: 8
$ species           <fct> Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adel…
$ island            <fct> Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgerse…
$ bill_length_mm    <dbl> 39.1, 39.5, 40.3, NA, 36.7, 39.3, 38.9, 39.2, 34.1, …
$ bill_depth_mm     <dbl> 18.7, 17.4, 18.0, NA, 19.3, 20.6, 17.8, 19.6, 18.1, …
$ flipper_length_mm <int> 181, 186, 195, NA, 193, 190, 181, 195, 193, 190, 186…
$ body_mass_g       <int> 3750, 3800, 3250, NA, 3450, 3650, 3625, 4675, 3475, …
$ sex               <fct> male, female, female, NA, female, male, female, male…
$ year              <int> 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007…



Our task is to calculate the mean value of several columns in this data frame. We show how to do this without a for loop and with a for loop.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
text:
A Tidyverse Shortcut: across()
We just wrote a for loop to avoid copy/paste, and it works!
BUT you might recall that dplyr has a function that does exactly this in one line: across() applies the same function to every selected column, with no for loop required

penguins %>%
  summarize(across(where(is.numeric), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))


Notice: where(is.numeric) selects only the numeric columns — so species, island, and sex are skipped automatically, instead of quietly returning NA like our for loop did.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
text:
Why output[i] and penguins[[i]]?

output <- vector(mode = "double", length = ncol(penguins))

for (i in seq_along(penguins)) {
  output[i] <- mean(penguins[[i]], na.rm = TRUE)
}

output

The object output is a vector where we store the loop results:

To access elements of a vector, use single square brackets []
So output[i] assigns the computed value into the i-th position of that vector

The object penguins is a dataframe (or tibble):

But mean() needs a numeric vector! To access a dataframe column as a vector, use double brackets [[]]

So penguins[[i]] gets the i-th column of the dataframe, and turns it into a vector

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
text:
Why output[i] and penguins[[i]]?

is.vector(penguins[[3]])           # TRUE (the raw vector)
is.data.frame(penguins[3])         # TRUE (a 1-col tibble/data frame)

mean(penguins[[3]], na.rm = TRUE)  # works, correct type (vector)
mean(penguins[3], na.rm = TRUE)    # error, wrong type (1-col tibble)





With data frames: [[ ]] returns the column itself as a vector; [ ] returns a one-column data frame
With vectors: [] and [[ ]] behave the same, but is best to use [] when subsetting vectors

text:

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
text:
For Loop in Data frames
So far we’ve looped over vectors, one element at a time.
What about looping over data frames?
A data frame can be looped over row by row or column by column, and both are possible in R.
But since a data frame is really a collection of vectors (each variable is one), R defaults to looping over columns, so that’s what we’ll do here.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 16
section: Coming Next Week: Beyond For Loops with Data frames
text:
Coming Next Week: Beyond For Loops with Data frames
For loops make iteration explicit and are an important programming tool. You will encounter them in many programming languages.
But R is a VECTORIZED language, built to operate on a whole column at once rather than element by element.
That means it has functions that can do the same job as a for loop, just in a way that fits R’s strengths better. Next week we’ll explore them:

across() (dplyr): apply a function to many columns in one line
map() (purrr): apply a function to every element of a list or column
apply() family (base R): the classic vectorized alternative to for loops

Let’s examine this code…

i will index each column
output[i] saves the results in the output vector at the correct position

section: Last Lecture: we concluded with this slide

Last Lecture: we concluded with this slide

But R is a VECTORIZED language, built to operate on a whole dataframe column at once rather than element by element.
That means it has functions that can do the same job as a for loop, just in a way that fits R’s strengths better. Next week we’ll explore them:

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: Why We Even Learn Loops in R?
text:
Why We Even Learn Loops in R?
Many tasks in R can and should be done without writing a for loop.
So why do we even learn loops in R? Because understanding loops is important:

to build logic and control flow skills
for non-vectorized tasks**, like row-by-row operations
to build more complex workflows
to be able to read and understand other people code
for general literacy on programming


Even if you don’t use loops every day, knowing how they work will strengthen your skills as coder.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: Three Main Alternatives to For Loops in R
text:
Three Main Alternatives to For Loops in R
When working with dataframes, R provides the following alternatives to for loop:



Function Family
Package
Best Used For
Docs




map_*()
purrr
Applying functions to elements of lists or columns
map docs


across()
dplyr
Applying the same function to multiple columns inside mutate() or summarize()
across docs


*apply()
base R
Row/column-wise operations on matrices or data frames
apply docs




The asterisk * is a placeholder for a family of related functions (e.g., map_dbl(), map_chr(), sapply(), etc.)

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: Option 1: Replacing for loops with map() functions
text:
Option 1: Replacing for loops with map() functions

The first option to replace a for loop is the family of map_*() functions from the purrr package.
What it does: applies a function to each element of a list or vector (for a data frame: each column), and returns the results in the type of output you choose
Best use: flexible, all-purpose iteration; unlike across(), it does not need to live inside a dplyr verb like summarize() or mutate()

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: Option 1: Replacing for loops with map() functions
text:
Option 1: Replacing for loops with map() functions
The so-called “map functions” come from the purr package in R: https://purrr.tidyverse.org/reference/map.html
There are several map_*() functions each creates a different type of output:

map() makes a list
map_lgl() makes a logical vector
map_int() makes an integer vector
map_dbl() makes a double vector
map_chr() makes a character vector

This is the same idea as in the for loop when we specify the mode of our output vector.
Let’s see a few examples using the penguins data.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: Option 2: Replacing for loops with across()
text:
Option 2: Replacing for loops with across()
Another option to replace a for loop is the across() function from dplyr
What it does: applies the same operation (e.g., mean) to multiple columns at once
Best use: since it comes from dplyr, it works seamlessly inside dplyr verbs like mutate() and summarize() which these are its favorite verbs to work with! You might remember this function from Lecture 7

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: Option 2: Replacing for loops with across()
text:
Option 2: Replacing for loops with across()
This example calculates the mean of several columns in a data frame. We used it in Lecture 16, when we rewrote it using a for loop:

penguins |> summarize(avg_bill_length = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))
penguins |> summarize(avg_bill_depth = mean(bill_depth_mm, na.rm = TRUE))
penguins |> summarize(avg_flipper_length = mean(flipper_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))
penguins |> summarize(avg_body_mass = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE))

Now, we rewrite it again but this time using across()

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: Option 2: Replacing for loops with across()
text:
Option 2: Replacing for loops with across()
Rewrite previous example using across():

penguins |> 
  summarize(
    across(
      .cols = where(is.numeric),           # select columns by type
      .fns = ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))    # apply mean, skipping NA


The function across() has two main arguments:

.cols: the columns to operate on. You can select them by position, name, or type - in this example, by type using where(is.numeric)
.fns: the function, or list of functions, to apply to each column (in this example, we use mean() and .x is a placeholder for the current column being processed)

You can omit .cols and .fns if you pass the arguments in the correct order, like that: penguins |> summarize(across(where(is.numeric), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: Option 2: Replacing for loops with across()
text:
Option 2: Replacing for loops with across()
Tip: you can also add names to the newly computed means to improve readabilty

penguins |> 
  summarize(
    across(
      .cols = where(is.numeric),                   
      .fns = ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE),             
      .names = "avg_{.col}"                   
    )
  )


What does \"avg_{.col}\" mean?

"avg_" is a literal prefix that will be added to each new column name.

{.col} is a placeholder that will be replaced with the original column name.

So if you’re applying mean() to a column named bill_length_mm, the result will be named avg_bill_length_mm

Option 3: Replacing for loops with apply() functions
Finally, a third option to replace a for loop in R is using one of the apply() functions from base R
What it does: applies the same operation (e.g., mean) across the rows or the columns of a data frame
Best use: when working outside tidyverse verbs (this is from base R!); applying a function across columns is the most common use

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: Option 3: Replacing for loops with apply() functions
text:
Option 3: Replacing for loops with apply() functions
We looked at this example earlier (which calculates the mean of several columns in a data frame) and rewrote it using a for loop. Now we rewrite it again but this time using apply():

penguins |> summarize(avg_bill_length = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))
penguins |> summarize(avg_bill_depth = mean(bill_depth_mm, na.rm = TRUE))
penguins |> summarize(avg_flipper_length = mean(flipper_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))
penguins |> summarize(avg_body_mass = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE))

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: Option 3: Replacing for loops with apply() functions
text:
Option 3: Replacing for loops with apply() functions
Rewrite the previous example using apply():

# manually select only numeric column using base R syntax
penguins_numeric <- penguins[ , c("bill_length_mm", "bill_depth_mm", 
                                  "flipper_length_mm", "body_mass_g")]

# apply mean to these columns
apply(penguins_numeric, 2, mean, na.rm = TRUE)  

Note the 2 stays for MARGIN = 2 (apply on the columns). If you write 1 it means rows.
Type ?apply in your Console for more info.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: Option 3: Replacing for loops with apply() functions
text:
Option 3: Replacing for loops with apply() functions
The apply() family refers to several related functions you can use:










Function
Use Case
Input Type
Output Type
Why Use It




apply()
Apply a function across rows or columns
Matrix, data frame
Vector or matrix
For row/column-wise operations


lapply()
Apply a function to each element
List, data frame
List
Keeps outputs as list (safe, flexible)


sapply()
Like lapply() but simplifies output
List, data frame
Vector or matrix
Shorter output, easier to read


vapply()
Like sapply(), but requires output type
List, data frame
Vector or matrix
Safer than sapply() (explicit type checking)


tapply()
Apply a function to vector values by group
Vector + Factor(s)
Vector
For group-wise summaries (e.g., mean by sex)




You don’t need to memorize each one — but remember there are multiple apply() options (like there are multiple map() options). Look them up and pick the one that fits your task!

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: Option 3: Replacing for loops with apply() functions
text:
Option 3: Replacing for loops with apply() functions

# Use numeric columns from penguins dataset
num_data <- penguins[ , 3:6]

# apply(): function across columns (MARGIN = 2)
apply(num_data, 2, mean, na.rm = TRUE)

# lapply(): list output
lapply(num_data, mean, na.rm = TRUE)

# sapply(): simplified vector output
sapply(num_data, mean, na.rm = TRUE)

# vapply(): safe version of sapply()
vapply(num_data, mean, na.rm = TRUE, FUN.VALUE = numeric(1))







Tips for the apply() Family



apply() works best with matrix-like structures

lapply() is list-safe, but sapply() is more concise
vapply() avoids surprises (e.g. unpredictable output type) by enforcing output type (here returns a single numeric value)

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
text:
Definition of While Loops
We focused on “for loops” because they are the most common, but R, and many other programming languages, also supports “while loops”
How while loops work:

Evaluate a condition
If the condition is TRUE, run the loop body
Start over: re-evaluate the condition
Repeat until the condition turns into FALSE (then the while loop is over)

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
text:
While Loop Examples
Take the previous code, but this time print counter also at the end:
counter <- 1

while(counter <= 3) {
  print(counter)
  counter <- counter + 1
  print(counter)
}
Why are the results different from the previous code?

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
text:
While Loop Examples
Observe this last one. What is the output of this code?
counter <- 1
while(counter < 4){
  print(counter)
  multiply <- counter * 100
  print(multiply)
  counter <- counter + 1
  print(counter)
}

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
text:
While Loop Uses
While Loops are useful when you don’t know in advance how many times to iterate: you want the loop to continue until a condition is met.
Example use cases:

Keep looping until you get three heads in a row from random coin flips

Keep accepting user input until you reach a target number of responses

While loops typically require a counter variable: set it outside the loop, and update it inside the loop, otherwise the loop never ends.
While loops are important but less common than for loops, especially for the types of tasks we do in this course.

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
text:
From using to writing functions
Today we learn the following concepts:

Using existing functions
Writing your own function
The return statement
stop()

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
text:
Using existing functions
Functions are all over R! We have been using functions from day 1 of this course. For example:

a <- c(1:5)
a

sum(a)
mean(a)

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
text:
What is a function?
A function:

is a block of code that accomplishes a task
has a name that typically describes the task, like sum() or mean()
takes one or more inputs arguments), processes them, and returns one or more outputs

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
text:
Using existing functions
To use a function, we need to know its arguments.
In the console, type help() with the function’s name in parenthesis to learn more about a function behavior and its arguments:
help(sum)
help(mean)

Some functions come from base R, like sum() or mean()
Other functions belong to specific packages, like summarize() or mutate() which belong to dplyr

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
text:
Writing custom functions
Other than using functions created by others, we can also write our own functions!
To do that we need the following:

name: something that describes the task that the function performs
arguments (input): at least one data structure (e.g., vector, etc.)
body: self-contained lines of code that manipulate the input, defining what the function does
return (output): statement that returns the result of the function’s calculations

Syntax:
name <- function(arg1, arg2, ...) { 
    result <- body with code that does things using arg1, arg2, etc.
    return(result)
    }

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
text:
Writing custom functions
To use a function, we need to call it with specific values.
In the example from the previous slide, our custom function my_sum() takes one argument, vector. When we call the function, this will be a specific vector, such as x, y, etc.

x <- c(2,1,5,2)
my_sum(vector = x)

y <- c(4,3,7)
my_sum(y)

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
text:
Advantages of writing custom functions
We wrote a sum function to illustrate how user-written functions work, but typically if there is already a built-in function in R , it’s best to use it (vs. rewriting it)
Instead write your own function when…

there is no a built-in function
you want to reduce repetitive code and chances for mistakes
you want to reuse code
you want organize code (e.g., one function imports the data, another cleans the data, etc.)

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
text:
Tips for writing functions: name
Use unique names:

If your function name matches the name of an existing R function, your function will replace the existing function for your current session (e.g., write my_sum, do not re-write sum)
Unless there is a specific reason (e.g. learning purposes, etc.), do not create a new function like my_sum() when R already has a built-in sum() function you can use

Use informative names:

The name should suggest what the function does
It should be short
Avoid reserved words like if, else, for, function, etc. To see the full list: type help(reserved) in the Console

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: Last Lecture we ended with this slide
text:
Last Lecture we ended with this slide
For loops make iteration explicit and are an important programming tool. You will encounter them in many programming languages.
But R is a VECTORIZED language, built to operate on a whole dataframe column at once rather than element by element.
That means it has functions that can do the same job as a for loop, just in a way that fits R’s strengths better. Next week we’ll explore them:

across() (dplyr): apply a function to many columns in one line
map() (purrr): apply a function to every element of a list or column
apply() family (base R): the classic vectorized alternative to for loops

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: Option 1: Replacing for loops with map() functions
text:
Option 1: Replacing for loops with map() functions
To use these properly, you need to pick the appropriate map() function (see previous slide) and specify at least two main arguments:

what you are iterating over
what you are calculating

Here we are calculating the mean of all numeric columns of the penguins dataframe:

penguins |> 
  select(where(is.numeric)) |>
  map_dbl(mean, na.rm = TRUE)

what does select(where(is.numeric)) do? And why we picked map_dbl() here?

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: 💻 Option 1 Practice: using functions from the map family
text:
💻 Option 1 Practice: using functions from the map family
Copy and paste this code (from the previous slide) into R and run it:
library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)

penguins |> 
  select(where(is.numeric)) |>
  map_dbl(mean, na.rm = TRUE)
Your tasks:

Modify the code to compute the median (instead of the mean) of each numeric column
In the original code, replace map_dbl() with map(). What changes in the output?

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
text:
💻 Option 2 Practice: using across
Copy and paste this code (from the previous slide) into R and run it:
penguins |> 
  summarize(
    across(
      .cols = where(is.numeric),                   
      .fns = ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE),             
      .names = "avg_{.col}"                   
    )
  )
Your tasks:

Compute these means separately for each species. Hint: add one dplyr verb before summarize()
Change the code to compute the median, and rename the new columns "med_{.col}"
Bonus: apply the mean only to the columns whose name ends with "_mm". Hint: Select columns by name patterns instead of type with ends_with() in .cols

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: 💻 Option 3 Practice: using functions from the apply family
text:
💻 Option 3 Practice: using functions from the apply family
Copy and paste this code into your script and run it:
num_data <- penguins[ , 3:6]
sapply(num_data, median, na.rm = TRUE)
Your tasks:

Rewrite the code above using lapply(). How is the output different?
A common pitfall! Run apply() on the full dataset: apply(penguins, 2, mean, na.rm = TRUE). What do you get, and why? Hint: apply() first converts its input to a matrix, and a matrix can hold only one data type. What happens when a data frame mixes numbers and text?

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 18
section: Using stop() when writing a function
text:
Using stop() when writing a function
Most functions exit in one of two ways. They either return() a value, indicating success, or they stop() and throw an error, indicating failure. Therefore:

return(): exits the function NORMALLY, handing back a value
stop(): exits the function WITH AN ERROR, so execution halts, the console shows Error:, and no misleading value is returned

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 19
text:
Anonymous Functions
Anonymous functions are functions without a name
When to use them:

When the function is very short and you need it only once
When another function (like those in the apply() family) expects another function as input

Whenever you can: write the function explicitly instead!

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 19
section: Anonymous Functions with a number as input
text:
Anonymous Functions with a number as input
Imagine we have the following function:

add <- function(x) {
  return(x + 3) }
add(2)

Re-write it as an anonymous function. Note: one line, no name, and call it with ():

(function(x) {x + 3}) (2)

Often anonymous functions are written without the {}, like this:

(function(x) x + 3) (2)

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 19
section: Anonymous Functions with a vector as input
text:
Anonymous Functions with a vector as input
Same function as before but here we pass a vector as input rather than a single number:

add <- function(x) {
  return(x + 3) }
add(c(1,2,3))

To rewrite it as anonymous function, the following code won’t work because the vector isn’t passed correctly:
(function(x) x + 3) c(1, 2, 3)
Instead, this works correctly, since the entire vector is passed as a single argument:

(function(x) x + 3) (c(1, 2, 3))

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 19
section: Anonymous Functions with sapply() and lapply()
text:
Anonymous Functions with sapply() and lapply()
You can also rewrite the previous code using sapply() or lapply(). Remember them from last time? They can be used instead of for loops in R.
Both belong to the apply() family and have the same basic structure:
lapply(X, FUN)
sapply(X, FUN)

X is a vector or list (the input)
FUN is a function to apply to each element of X

They both loop through the elements of X, apply FUN to each element…

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 19
section: Anonymous Functions with sapply() and lapply()
text:
Anonymous Functions with sapply() and lapply()
…But then differ in how they return the result:

lapply means “list apply”, always returns the output as a list
sapply() means “simplify apply”, returns a simplified version of the list (usually a vector, sometimes a matrix)


# previous anonymous function
(function(x) x + 1) (c(1, 2, 3))

# re-written with sapply
sapply(c(1,2,3), function(x) x + 1)

# re-written with lapply
lapply(c(1,2,3), function(x) x + 1)

Tip: Using sapply() is more common because it returns a vector, which is easier to work with that a list!

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 19
text:
Anonymous Functions the purrr way
The purrr package (remember the map(), map_dbl() from last lecture? same package!) provides another way to write anonymous functions.
Same function as before that adds 1 to each element of the input:

add <- function(x) {
  x + 1
}
add(c(1,2,3))

Rewrite it as anonymous function in purrr in two ways:

library(purrr)
map_dbl(c(1, 2, 3), function(x) x + 1)
map_dbl(c(1,2,3), ~.x + 1)

Tip: map() has several versions like map_dbl(), map_chr(), and map_df() which let you control the output type directly

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 19
text:
Functions should be well-documented! How?
Each function should include the following:

a one-sentence description of the function summarizing what it does
all function’s arguments, denoted as Args:, with their data types
a description of the return value, denoted by Returns:

Do not use AI do generate your documentation: it overly complicate things

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 19
text:
Codying style: document your function

my_sum <- function(vector) {
  
  # Calculates the sum of all numbers in a numeric vector using a `for` loop.
  # Args:
  #   vector (num): a numeric vector whose elements will be summed
  # Returns:
  #  value (num): single numeric value.
  # Example use:
  #   x <- c(3, 1, 4)
  #   my_sum(x)
    
  total <- 0
  for (i in seq_along(vector)) {
    total <- total + vector[i]
  }
  total
}

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 19
text:
Functions and {{ }}
You can write custom functions and use them together with dplyr or ggplot functions!
You need the following elements:

syntax to write a custom function
special operators that allows R to evaluate your custom functions correctly:

bang-bang !! with enquo() (this is the old method)
curly-curly or embracing { } (this replaced the former method!)


Check out Chapter 25 for details https://r4ds.hadley.nz/functions.html

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 19
text:
Functions and {{ }}
Example: create a data frame of age and height of 5 individuals:

library(dplyr)

df <- tibble(age = c(10, 12, 13, 14, 10), 
             height = c(110, 140, 155, 170, 130))
df

# A tibble: 5 × 2
    age height
  <dbl>  <dbl>
1    10    110
2    12    140
3    13    155
4    14    170
5    10    130

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 19
text:
Functions and {{ }}
Use summarize() from dplyr to compute the mean and standard deviation on the column age of this data frame:

df %>%
  summarize(mean_age = mean(age),
            sd_age = sd(age))

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  mean_age sd_age
     <dbl>  <dbl>
1     11.8   1.79

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 19
Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 19
text:
Functions and {{ }}
Embracing a variable means to wrap it in braces so var becomes { var } when using functions dplyr or ggplot code.
It tells dplyr to use the value stored inside the argument, not the argument as the literal variable name
From the book: “one way to remember what’s happening is to think of { } as looking down a tunnel — { var } will make a dplyr function look inside of var rather than looking for a variable called var”

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 19
text:
Functions and {{ }}
Rewrite it with multiple columns as arguments:

custom_summary <- function(data, col1, col2) {
  results <- list(
    summarise(data, 
              mean_value = mean({{ col1 }}),
              sd_value = sd({{ col1 }})),
    summarise(data, 
              mean_value = mean({{ col2 }}),
              sd_value = sd({{ col2 }}))
  )
  # Combine output into a single data frame
  bind_rows(results)
}
custom_summary(df, age, height)

# A tibble: 2 × 2
  mean_value sd_value
       <dbl>    <dbl>
1       11.8     1.79
2      141      23.0

title: Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 19
text:
Functions and {{ }}
When do you need to embrace when writing a function?
In all cases of “tidy evaluation,” which has two main types (see the readings, Chapter 25 for details):

Data Masking: in functions such as arrange(), filter(), summarize() from dplyr and in aes() from ggplot2 often when you need to pass a variable name
Tidy Selection: in functions such as select(), relocate(), rename() when you need to select variables by name
